era_context: The Ming Dynasty is in the midst of the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402), with the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) challenging his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor. The Ottoman Empire is at the height of its power under Bayezid I. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War continues; the Western Schism in the Catholic Church persists. The Italian Renaissance is beginning to flourish, with humanist scholars and artists reshaping European culture. This year marks the beginning of the 15th century.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited written records)

events.title: Owain Glyndŵr Declares Himself Prince of Wales; Rebellion Begins
events.description: Owain Glyndŵr (c. 1359–c. 1415), a Welsh nobleman, declares himself Prince of Wales and launches a rebellion against English rule. The rebellion is triggered by a personal dispute with an English neighbor, but it quickly becomes a nationalist uprising against English authority. Glyndŵr's rebellion will last until 1415 and will be one of the last major Welsh uprisings against English rule. The rebellion attracts support from various Welsh lords and common people, and at its height, Glyndŵr controls much of Wales. The rebellion poses a significant challenge to Henry IV's authority and diverts English military resources.
events.certainty_note: Glyndŵr's rebellion is attested in contemporary English chronicles and later Welsh sources. The date of 1400 is generally accepted for the beginning of the rebellion.

events.title: Jingnan Rebellion Begins; Yongle Emperor Challenges Jianwen Emperor
events.description: The Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402) begins, with the Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di), Prince of Yan, challenging his nephew, the Jianwen Emperor, for control of the Ming Dynasty. The rebellion is triggered by the Jianwen Emperor's attempts to reduce the power of the regional princes, which threatens the Yongle Emperor's position. The Yongle Emperor raises an army and marches on Nanjing, the capital. The rebellion will last three years and will result in the Yongle Emperor's victory and consolidation of power. This rebellion is one of the most significant civil conflicts in Ming history and has profound implications for the dynasty's future.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Jingnan Rebellion is well-documented in Ming administrative records and later historical compilations. The date of 1399 is sometimes given for the start of the rebellion, but 1400 is also used.

events.title: Henry IV of England Consolidates Power; Early Reign Challenges
events.description: Henry IV of England (r. 1399–1413) is in his first year of rule after deposing Richard II in 1399. By 1400, Henry is working to consolidate his power and establish his legitimacy. He faces multiple challenges: the Glyndŵr rebellion in Wales, baronial opposition, religious dissent (Lollards), and questions about the legitimacy of his claim to the throne. Despite these challenges, Henry is gradually establishing his authority. The year 1400 marks the beginning of a reign characterized by constant vigilance against potential rivals and rebels.
events.certainty_note: Henry IV's consolidation of power is well-documented in contemporary English sources.

events.title: Ottoman Empire at Peak Power; Bayezid I Rules
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402) is at the height of its power and territorial expansion. By 1400, the Ottomans control much of Anatolia and the Balkans, and they are threatening Constantinople, the last major Byzantine stronghold. Bayezid I is known as 'the Thunderbolt' (Yildirim) for his military prowess and rapid campaigns. However, by 1400, Timur's invasion is imminent, and within two years, Bayezid will be defeated at the Battle of Ankara and captured. The Ottoman Empire will be temporarily fragmented, but it will eventually recover and become the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's rule and the Ottoman Empire's power are well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.

events.title: Venice Maintains Maritime Dominance; Commercial Networks Expand
events.description: Venice, the dominant maritime power in the Mediterranean, is maintaining and expanding its commercial networks. The Republic is at the height of its power and influence, with a sophisticated commercial and financial system. Venice's wealth is based on its control of Mediterranean trade, particularly the spice trade from the East. By 1400, Venice is one of the wealthiest and most powerful city-states in Europe. The Republic is engaged in diplomatic negotiations with various powers and is securing trade routes and establishing trading posts throughout the Mediterranean and beyond.

sources.name: Venetian state archives (Archivio di Stato di Venezia)

events.certainty_note: Venetian commercial and political dominance is well-documented in state archives and contemporary sources.

events.title: Italian Renaissance Flourishes; Humanist Scholarship Advances
events.description: The Italian Renaissance is flourishing in 1400, with humanist scholars and artists reshaping European culture. Florence is the center of this cultural movement, with the Medici family beginning to emerge as patrons of the arts and learning. Humanist scholars are recovering and studying classical texts, and artists are developing new techniques in painting, sculpture, and architecture. The Renaissance represents a revival of interest in classical learning and a shift away from medieval scholasticism. By 1400, the movement is well-established in Italy and is beginning to spread to other parts of Europe.

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari's Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The Italian Renaissance is well-documented in contemporary sources and later historical accounts.

events.title: Portuguese Exploration Begins; Prince Henry the Navigator's Early Patronage
events.description: Portuguese exploration of the Atlantic Ocean is beginning under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator (1394–1460). By 1400, Portuguese expeditions have reached the Canary Islands and are beginning to explore the West African coast. These voyages are motivated by commercial interests (gold, spices, slaves) and religious zeal (conversion of non-Christians). The Portuguese are developing new maritime technologies and navigation techniques that will eventually enable the Age of Exploration. Prince Henry's patronage of exploration will continue for decades and will establish Portugal as a leading maritime power.

sources.name: Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea by Gomes Eanes de Zurara

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is attested in later chronicles; specific dates for individual voyages in 1400 are uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the beginning of the Jingnan Rebellion is sometimes given as 1399 rather than 1400; modern scholarship generally accepts 1399 as the start date, with 1400 being the year of major military campaigns. Owain Glyndŵr's rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a purely nationalist uprising, but it was also driven by personal grievances and feudal disputes. The significance of the rebellion is sometimes overstated; while it was a major uprising, it did not fundamentally alter the balance of power between Wales and England. The Italian Renaissance is sometimes portrayed as a sudden break from the medieval period, but it was actually a gradual process that had been developing for decades. Prince Henry the Navigator's role in Portuguese exploration is sometimes romanticized; he was one patron among many, and Portuguese exploration was driven by multiple economic and political factors.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1400 is moderate for Europe and China, with administrative records and chronicles available. Ottoman sources are less abundant but still available. Italian sources are detailed regarding Florence and Venice. African, American, and Oceanic history remains almost entirely absent from surviving written records. The sources that do exist are often produced by and for the ruling classes, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people, women, and non-literate societies. Chinese sources may be biased toward the Yongle Emperor's legitimacy, while English sources may downplay the significance of Glyndŵr's rebellion. The Italian Renaissance is often portrayed through the lens of elite patrons and artists, potentially obscuring the experiences of common people and women.

graph_edges.note: Glyndŵr's rebellion begins in 1400 and continues until 1415
Jingnan Rebellion begins in 1400 and leads to Yongle Emperor's consolidation in 1402
Ottoman Empire's peak power in 1400 is followed by defeat at Ankara in 1402
Italian Renaissance flourishes in 1400 and continues to develop throughout the 15th century

era_context: The late medieval period in Europe, marked by the fragmentation of feudal authority and the rise of national monarchies. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty (established 1368) is consolidating control after the Mongol Yuan collapse. The Timurid Renaissance flourishes in Central Asia and Persia. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is at its height, and the Inca Empire is expanding under Pachacuti's successors.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented in Western sources)
Southeast Asia (sparse European documentation; local chronicles exist but underrepresented in English-language historiography)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (minimal written record)

events.title: Henry IV of England defeats and captures Richard II at Conway Castle; Richard's deposition formalized
events.description: Henry Bolingbroke, Duke of Lancaster, returns from exile to challenge Richard II's rule. After a military campaign, Richard is forced to surrender at Conway Castle in North Wales. Richard is deposed and Henry is crowned Henry IV, establishing the House of Lancaster on the English throne. This event marks the beginning of the Wars of the Roses (indirectly) and represents a major shift in English royal succession away from direct primogeniture. Richard dies in captivity in 1400, likely murdered.
key_figures: Henry Bolingbroke (Duke of Lancaster, later Henry IV)
Thomas Mowbray (Duke of Norfolk, supporter of Henry)

sources.name: Froissart, Chronicles (compiled early 15th century, based on earlier accounts)
Traison et Mort de Richard Deux (contemporary French account)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles document this event with consistent dating. The deposition parliament occurred in September 1399.
cross_references: 1485-001 (Battle of Bosworth Field, end of Wars of the Roses)

events.title: Timur (Tamerlane) dies in Samarkand; Timurid Empire begins fragmentation
events.description: Timur, the Turco-Mongol conqueror who built a vast empire stretching from the Mediterranean to the borders of China, dies in February 1399 at Samarkand. His death marks the beginning of the rapid fragmentation of his empire among his sons and grandsons. However, the Timurid cultural and artistic legacy persists, particularly under his grandson Ulugh Beg, leading to a flourishing of Persian and Islamic art, astronomy, and literature in the 15th century. The empire's military dominance ends, but its cultural influence endures.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (compiled early 15th century, based on earlier sources and oral tradition)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur (written mid-15th century)

events.certainty_note: Timur's death in February 1399 is well-attested in multiple sources. The exact date (February 18 or 19) is traditional but consistent across sources.

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming court; preparation for future voyages
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, is rising in prominence at the court of the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424). Though his famous voyages begin in 1405, by 1399 he is already a trusted military and administrative figure. The Ming Dynasty is consolidating its power after the Jianwen Emperor's accession in 1398. Zheng He's position reflects the Yongle faction's growing influence, which will culminate in the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (then Prince of Yan, planning usurpation)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Records, compiled contemporaneously)
Luo Maodeng, Zheng He's biography (compiled later, based on official records)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact role in 1399 is not precisely documented. He is known to have been active in the Yongle faction's military campaigns (1399–1402), but specific events for 1399 are inferred from broader chronologies.

events.title: Jianwen Emperor's reign faces internal instability; Yongle faction consolidates power
events.description: The Jianwen Emperor, who ascended in 1398 at age 21, faces mounting pressure from his uncle, the Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor). The Jianwen Emperor's attempts to reduce princely power through the 'Removal of the Princes' (Jingnan) campaign provoke resistance. By 1399, the Prince of Yan is openly preparing for rebellion, which will erupt in 1402. This period represents a critical moment of dynastic instability within the Ming, though the dynasty itself remains stable.
events.certainty_note: The political tensions of 1399 are well-documented in Ming records. The Jianwen Emperor's reign (1398–1402) is thoroughly chronicled.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates control in the Balkans; Bayezid I expands territory
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), continues Ottoman expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia. By 1399, the Ottomans control much of the Balkans, including parts of Serbia, Bulgaria, and Greece. Bayezid is consolidating Ottoman power and preparing for further expansion. The Ottoman military system, based on the Janissary corps and feudal cavalry (Sipahi), is becoming increasingly formidable. This period marks the Ottoman Empire's emergence as a major regional power.

sources.name: Byzantine sources (contemporary accounts of Ottoman expansion)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1399 is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine sources. Specific military campaigns for 1399 are less precisely dated than major battles.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Henry IV's deposition of Richard II is sometimes overstated as a 'revolution' in English governance; modern scholarship emphasizes that it was a factional coup within the nobility, not a fundamental break with feudal monarchy. The Wars of the Roses (1455–1487) are often attributed to Richard's deposition, but this is a post-hoc narrative; the actual causes were more complex and rooted in the weakness of Henry VI (r. 1422–1461), not Richard's deposition. Timur's death in 1399 is sometimes dated to 1405 in older sources, but modern scholarship confirms February 1399. The Timurid 'Renaissance' is sometimes romanticized as a unified cultural movement; in reality, it was fragmented and regionally variable, with significant violence and instability during the succession crisis.
historiographic_note: The year 1399 is well-documented in European sources (particularly English and Ottoman), moderately documented in Chinese sources, and less well-documented in African, Southeast Asian, and American sources. European sources are heavily biased toward political and military events; cultural and economic history are underrepresented. Chinese sources are official and court-centric, potentially obscuring local and popular history. Ottoman sources are largely compiled after the fact (15th–16th centuries) and may reflect later political narratives. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific does not mean nothing significant occurred; it reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records and modern historiography.

graph_edges.note: Henry IV's deposition of Richard II led to Richard's death in captivity in 1400.
Timur's death in 1399 weakened the Timurid Empire, indirectly contributing to Bayezid I's confidence before his defeat by Timur's successor in 1402.
The Jianwen Emperor's political instability in 1399 directly led to the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
Ottoman expansion under Bayezid I was occurring simultaneously with the Yongle usurpation in China, representing parallel processes of imperial consolidation.

era_context: The late medieval period continues with significant dynastic transitions across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty in China is transitioning from the Hongwu Emperor (d. 1398) to his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War between England and France is in a period of relative peace (truce period). The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia and the Balkans. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is expanding, and the Inca Empire is in a period of consolidation.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written record)
Americas (limited written records; Aztec and Inca records exist but are underrepresented in Western historiography)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor dies; Jianwen Emperor ascends to Ming throne
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398), founder of the Ming Dynasty, dies on June 24, 1398, at age 71. His grandson, the Jianwen Emperor (age 21), ascends to the throne. The Hongwu Emperor had designated his grandson as heir, bypassing his eldest son (who had died in 1392). This succession is controversial among the imperial princes, particularly the Prince of Yan (the Hongwu Emperor's fourth son), who will challenge the Jianwen Emperor's authority and eventually usurp the throne in 1402. The Jianwen Emperor inherits a centralized, militarized state but faces immediate challenges to his legitimacy.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Prince of Yan, r. 1402–1424, uncle)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Official Ming Records, compiled contemporaneously)
Sima Guang, Zizhi Tongjian (comprehensive history, compiled 11th century but covering this period)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's death on June 24, 1398, and the Jianwen Emperor's accession are precisely documented in Ming records. This is one of the most securely dated events in Chinese history.

events.title: Jianwen Emperor begins 'Removal of the Princes' (Jingnan) campaign to reduce princely power
events.description: Shortly after his accession, the Jianwen Emperor, advised by his minister Huang Zicheng, begins a systematic campaign to reduce the power of the imperial princes. This campaign, known as the 'Jingnan' (removal of the princes), involves stripping princes of their military commands and administrative authority. The campaign provokes resistance from the Prince of Yan and other princes, destabilizing the early years of the Jianwen Emperor's reign. This policy directly contributes to the Prince of Yan's rebellion in 1402.
key_figures: Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor, primary target)
events.certainty_note: The Jianwen Emperor's 'Removal of the Princes' campaign is well-documented in Ming records. It began in 1398 and continued until 1402.

events.title: Timur prepares final military campaign against Ming China; dies before departure
events.description: Timur, the aging Turco-Mongol conqueror, begins preparations for a massive military campaign against the Ming Dynasty in China. He mobilizes a large army and plans to march eastward. However, he dies in February 1399 before the campaign can be launched. This planned invasion would have been one of the largest military expeditions of the medieval world, but it never occurs. The death of Timur marks the end of an era of Turco-Mongol conquest and the beginning of the fragmentation of his empire.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler, would have faced invasion)
events.certainty_note: Timur's preparations for a campaign against China are attested in later sources, but the exact timing and scope are debated. The campaign was planned but never executed due to Timur's death in February 1399.
cross_references: 1405-001 (Zheng He's first voyage, in part a response to Timurid threats)

events.title: Bayezid I consolidates Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402) continues to consolidate Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans. By 1398, the Ottomans control much of the Balkans and are expanding in Anatolia at the expense of the declining Anatolian Beyliks (Turkish principalities). Bayezid's military campaigns and administrative reforms are strengthening the Ottoman state. The Ottoman Empire is becoming the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans, a position it will maintain for centuries.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in 1398 is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine sources. Specific events for 1398 are less precisely dated than major battles.

events.title: Hundred Years' War in truce period; English and French courts negotiate
events.description: The Hundred Years' War between England and France is in a period of relative peace and negotiation. The Treaty of Paris (1396) had established a 28-year truce between England and France. By 1398, both kingdoms are engaged in internal political struggles (Henry IV's rise in England, the Armagnac-Burgundian conflict in France) rather than major military campaigns against each other. This truce period allows both kingdoms to focus on internal consolidation.
events.certainty_note: The truce period of the Hundred Years' War (1396–1415) is well-documented in English and French sources.
cross_references: 1415-001 (Battle of Agincourt, resumption of major warfare)

disconfirming_evidence: The Jianwen Emperor's 'Removal of the Princes' campaign is sometimes portrayed as a rational policy to centralize power; modern scholarship suggests it was poorly executed and provoked unnecessary resistance. The campaign's effectiveness is debated—it weakened some princes but failed to neutralize the Prince of Yan, who was the most dangerous. Timur's planned invasion of China is sometimes exaggerated in scope; while it was a significant military preparation, it was not unprecedented in scale. The Hundred Years' War truce is sometimes portrayed as a period of complete peace; in reality, there were minor skirmishes and privateering, though no major campaigns.
historiographic_note: The year 1398 is very well-documented in Chinese sources (Ming Shilu provides detailed records), moderately documented in European sources, and less well-documented in Ottoman sources (which are largely compiled later). Chinese sources are official and court-centric. European sources are biased toward political and military events. Ottoman sources are retrospective and may reflect later political narratives. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Jianwen Emperor's accession in 1398 directly led to the 'Removal of the Princes' campaign.
The 'Removal of the Princes' campaign provoked the Prince of Yan's rebellion, which culminated in the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
Timur's preparations for invasion in 1398 were cut short by his death in February 1399.
The Hundred Years' War truce was ongoing during Henry IV's rise to power in England in 1399.

era_context: The late medieval period continues with the consolidation of major powers across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty in China is stable under the Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398). In Europe, the Hundred Years' War is in a truce period following the Treaty of Paris (1396). The Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is expanding, and the Inca Empire is consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written record)

events.title: Kalmar Union established; Denmark, Norway, and Sweden united under single monarch
events.description: Queen Margaret I of Denmark (r. 1375–1412) orchestrates the union of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden under a single monarch. The Kalmar Union is formally established in June 1397 at Kalmar, Sweden. Margaret's nephew, Eric of Pomerania, is crowned king of all three realms. This union, intended to strengthen Scandinavian power against the Hanseatic League and other rivals, lasts until 1523 (Sweden's separation). The Kalmar Union represents a significant consolidation of Nordic power and marks the beginning of a new era in Scandinavian politics.
key_figures: Margaret I of Denmark (r. 1375–1412, architect of union)
Eric of Pomerania (crowned king of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden)
Albrecht of Mecklenburg (previous king of Sweden, deposed)

sources.name: Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (compiled 12th century, but covering this period)

events.certainty_note: The Kalmar Union was formally established in June 1397. The date and the key figures are well-documented in Scandinavian sources.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming Dynasty; prepares succession
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398), now in his 70s, is consolidating the Ming Dynasty and preparing for succession. He has designated his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor, as heir (his eldest son died in 1392). The Hongwu Emperor is strengthening the central bureaucracy and military, establishing the foundations for the Ming Dynasty's long reign. He is also dealing with internal threats from the imperial princes, particularly the Prince of Yan, whom he suspects of disloyalty. The Hongwu Emperor's reign is marked by centralization, military strength, and cultural flourishing.
key_figures: Prince of Yan (fourth son, suspected of disloyalty)
events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of the Ming Dynasty is well-documented in Ming records. His death in 1398 is precisely dated.
cross_references: 1398-001 (Hongwu Emperor's death and Jianwen Emperor's accession)

events.title: Zheng He serves in Yongle faction's military campaigns in Yunnan
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, is serving in the military campaigns of the Prince of Yan (future Yongle Emperor) in Yunnan province. These campaigns are part of the Yongle faction's consolidation of power within the Ming Dynasty. Zheng He's military service and administrative skills are earning him recognition within the Yongle faction, positioning him for future prominence. By 1397, Zheng He is already a trusted military officer, though his most famous voyages will not begin until 1405.
events.certainty_note: Zheng He's exact role in 1397 is not precisely documented. He is known to have been active in the Yongle faction's military campaigns (1399–1402), but specific events for 1397 are inferred from broader chronologies.

events.title: Bayezid I defeats Anatolian Beyliks; Ottoman expansion continues
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402) continues his military campaigns against the Anatolian Beyliks (Turkish principalities). By 1397, the Ottomans have conquered or subjugated most of the major Beyliks, consolidating Ottoman control over Anatolia. This expansion is part of Bayezid's broader strategy to create a unified Ottoman state. The conquest of the Beyliks removes potential rivals and allows the Ottomans to focus on expansion in the Balkans and the Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia during the 1390s is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine sources. Specific military campaigns for 1397 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Treaty of Paris truce between England and France continues; relative peace in Western Europe
events.description: The Treaty of Paris (1396) established a 28-year truce between England and France in the Hundred Years' War. By 1397, this truce is holding, allowing both kingdoms to focus on internal consolidation. In England, Richard II is consolidating his power after the crisis of 1397 (the Appellants' revolt). In France, Charles VI is dealing with the early stages of the Armagnac-Burgundian conflict. The truce period is marked by relative peace in Western Europe, though tensions remain beneath the surface.
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Paris truce (1396–1415) is well-documented in English and French sources. The year 1397 falls within this truce period.

disconfirming_evidence: The Kalmar Union is sometimes portrayed as a unified Nordic state; in reality, it was a personal union under a single monarch, with each kingdom retaining its own laws, administration, and institutions. The union was often unstable, with Sweden and Norway frequently resisting Danish dominance. The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of the Ming Dynasty is sometimes portrayed as complete; in reality, the imperial princes remained a significant threat, as evidenced by the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402. Bayezid I's conquest of the Anatolian Beyliks is sometimes portrayed as a rapid, decisive process; in reality, it was gradual and involved complex diplomatic and military maneuvering.
historiographic_note: The year 1397 is well-documented in Scandinavian sources (particularly Danish and Swedish chronicles), well-documented in Chinese sources (Ming Shilu), and moderately documented in European sources. Scandinavian sources are official and court-centric. Chinese sources are official and court-centric. European sources are biased toward political and military events. Ottoman sources are largely compiled later and may reflect later political narratives. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Kalmar Union established in 1397 lasted until Sweden's separation in 1523.
The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation in 1397 set the stage for his death and the Jianwen Emperor's accession in 1398.
Zheng He's military service in the Yongle faction in 1397 contributed to his prominence during the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402.
Ottoman expansion under Bayezid I in 1397 was occurring simultaneously with the consolidation of the Ming Dynasty in China.

era_context: The late medieval period is marked by the consolidation of major powers across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty in China is stable under the Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398). In Europe, the Hundred Years' War is entering a period of truce following the Treaty of Paris. The Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is expanding, and the Inca Empire is consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written record)

events.title: Treaty of Paris establishes 28-year truce in Hundred Years' War
events.description: The Treaty of Paris, signed in March 1396, establishes a 28-year truce between England and France in the Hundred Years' War. The truce is brokered by the Papacy and is intended to allow both kingdoms to focus on other threats (particularly the Ottoman Empire). The treaty includes a marriage alliance: Richard II of England marries Isabella of Valois, daughter of Charles VI of France. This truce marks a significant shift in Anglo-French relations and allows both kingdoms to consolidate their power. The truce will hold until 1415, when Henry V of England resumes major military campaigns in France.
key_figures: Isabella of Valois (daughter of Charles VI, marries Richard II)
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Paris is precisely dated to March 1396 in English and French sources. The marriage of Richard II and Isabella of Valois is also well-documented.
cross_references: 1415-001 (Battle of Agincourt, resumption of major warfare)

events.title: Battle of Nicopolis; Ottoman victory over Crusader forces in the Balkans
events.description: On September 28, 1396, Bayezid I defeats a Crusader army led by Sigismund of Hungary at the Battle of Nicopolis (in modern-day Bulgaria). The Crusade, organized by Pope Boniface IX and supported by various European powers, was intended to check Ottoman expansion in the Balkans. The Ottoman victory is decisive and marks a major setback for European efforts to contain Ottoman power. The battle demonstrates the military superiority of the Ottoman Janissary corps and establishes the Ottomans as the dominant power in the Balkans. The defeat also discredits the Crusade movement and contributes to the shift toward the Treaty of Paris truce.
key_figures: Mircea the Elder (Wallachian voivode, ally of Sigismund)
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Nicopolis is precisely dated to September 28, 1396, and is well-documented in multiple sources. The Ottoman victory and the capture of Crusader nobles are confirmed.

events.title: Richard II consolidates power in England; crisis of 1397 begins
events.description: Richard II (r. 1377–1399) is consolidating his power in England after years of conflict with the nobility. In 1396, Richard is at the height of his power, having secured the Treaty of Paris and married Isabella of Valois. However, tensions with the nobility remain, particularly with the Appellants (nobles who had challenged his authority in 1388). In 1397, Richard will move against the Appellants, leading to the 'Revenge Parliament' and the exile of Henry Bolingbroke (future Henry IV). This period marks the beginning of the end for Richard's reign.
key_figures: Henry Bolingbroke (Duke of Lancaster, future Henry IV)
John of Gaunt (Duke of Lancaster, Henry's father)
events.certainty_note: Richard II's consolidation of power in 1396 is well-documented in English sources. The events of 1397 (the Revenge Parliament) are also well-documented.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming Dynasty; military and administrative reforms
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398) continues to consolidate the Ming Dynasty through military and administrative reforms. By 1396, the Ming Dynasty is stable and centralized, with a strong military and bureaucracy. The Hongwu Emperor is preparing for succession and dealing with threats from the imperial princes. The Ming Dynasty is experiencing a period of cultural flourishing, with significant achievements in art, literature, and architecture. The Hongwu Emperor's reign is marked by centralization, military strength, and cultural achievement.
events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of the Ming Dynasty is well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Timur consolidates Timurid Empire; prepares for final campaigns
events.description: Timur (r. 1369–1399), the aging Turco-Mongol conqueror, is consolidating his vast empire and preparing for final military campaigns. By 1396, Timur has conquered much of Central Asia, Persia, and parts of the Middle East. He is planning a major campaign against the Ottoman Empire and the Ming Dynasty. Timur's empire is at its height, but it is also showing signs of strain due to the vast distances and diverse populations. The empire will begin to fragment after Timur's death in 1399.
events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of his empire in 1396 is attested in later sources. Specific events for 1396 are less precisely dated than major battles.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Paris is sometimes portrayed as a complete peace; in reality, it was a truce that allowed both sides to prepare for future conflict. The treaty did not resolve the underlying territorial disputes between England and France. The Battle of Nicopolis is sometimes portrayed as a turning point in Ottoman-European relations; while it was a significant victory, it did not immediately lead to Ottoman dominance in Europe. European powers continued to resist Ottoman expansion, though with limited success. Richard II's consolidation of power in 1396 is sometimes portrayed as stable; in reality, it was fragile and dependent on the loyalty of the nobility, which he would lose in 1399.
historiographic_note: The year 1396 is well-documented in European sources (particularly English, French, and Hungarian), well-documented in Chinese sources (Ming Shilu), and moderately documented in Ottoman sources (largely compiled later). European sources are biased toward political and military events. Chinese sources are official and court-centric. Ottoman sources are retrospective and may reflect later political narratives. The Battle of Nicopolis is one of the most thoroughly documented events of 1396, with accounts from multiple European and Ottoman sources. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records.

graph_edges.note: The Treaty of Paris (March 1396) and the Battle of Nicopolis (September 1396) occurred in the same year, both marking significant shifts in European politics.
The Ottoman victory at Nicopolis in 1396 established Bayezid I as a major power, leading to his confrontation with Timur in 1402.
The Treaty of Paris truce (1396–1415) was broken by Henry V's invasion of France, leading to the Battle of Agincourt in 1415.
Richard II's consolidation of power in 1396 and his actions in 1397 (the Revenge Parliament) directly led to Henry IV's deposition of Richard in 1399.

era_context: The late medieval period is characterized by the consolidation of major powers across Eurasia. The Ming Dynasty in China is stable under the Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398). In Europe, the Hundred Years' War is entering a period of negotiation that will lead to the Treaty of Paris (1396). The Ottoman Empire is expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire is expanding, and the Inca Empire is consolidating power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islands and Oceania (no written record)

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming Dynasty; deals with princely threats
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (r. 1368–1398) continues to consolidate the Ming Dynasty and deal with threats from the imperial princes. By 1395, the Hongwu Emperor is in his late 60s and is preparing for succession. He has designated his grandson, the Jianwen Emperor, as heir (his eldest son died in 1392). The Hongwu Emperor is concerned about the power of the imperial princes, particularly the Prince of Yan, and is taking steps to limit their authority. The Ming Dynasty is stable and centralized, with a strong military and bureaucracy.
key_figures: Prince of Yan (fourth son, suspected of disloyalty)
events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of the Ming Dynasty is well-documented in Ming records. His concerns about the imperial princes are attested in official records.
cross_references: 1398-001 (Hongwu Emperor's death and Jianwen Emperor's accession)

events.title: Bayezid I consolidates Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402) continues to consolidate Ottoman power in Anatolia and the Balkans. By 1395, the Ottomans control much of the Balkans and are expanding in Anatolia at the expense of the declining Anatolian Beyliks. Bayezid's military campaigns and administrative reforms are strengthening the Ottoman state. The Ottoman Empire is becoming the dominant power in the eastern Mediterranean and the Balkans. Bayezid is preparing for further expansion and is planning military campaigns against the Crusaders and other rivals.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation in 1395 is attested in later Ottoman chronicles and contemporary Byzantine sources. Specific events for 1395 are less precisely dated than major battles.

events.title: Richard II negotiates peace with France; Treaty of Paris negotiations begin
events.description: Richard II of England (r. 1377–1399) is negotiating peace with France. These negotiations will lead to the Treaty of Paris in 1396, which establishes a 28-year truce in the Hundred Years' War. The negotiations are brokered by the Papacy and are intended to allow both kingdoms to focus on other threats. Richard is also negotiating a marriage alliance with France: he will marry Isabella of Valois, daughter of Charles VI. These negotiations mark a significant shift in Anglo-French relations and reflect Richard's desire to consolidate his power through diplomatic means.
key_figures: Isabella of Valois (daughter of Charles VI, future wife of Richard II)
events.certainty_note: The negotiations for the Treaty of Paris began in 1395 and were concluded in 1396. The exact timeline of negotiations is less precisely documented than the final treaty.

events.title: Timur consolidates Timurid Empire; military campaigns in Persia and the Caucasus
events.description: Timur (r. 1369–1399), the aging Turco-Mongol conqueror, is consolidating his vast empire through military campaigns in Persia and the Caucasus. By 1395, Timur has conquered much of Central Asia and Persia and is preparing for final campaigns against the Ottoman Empire and the Ming Dynasty. Timur's empire is at its height, but it is also showing signs of strain due to the vast distances and diverse populations. The empire will begin to fragment after Timur's death in 1399.
events.certainty_note: Timur's military campaigns in 1395 are attested in later sources. Specific events for 1395 are less precisely dated than major battles.

events.title: Hundred Years' War in negotiation phase; relative peace in Western Europe
events.description: The Hundred Years' War between England and France is in a negotiation phase. By 1395, both kingdoms are engaged in peace negotiations that will lead to the Treaty of Paris in 1396. The war has been ongoing since 1337, with periods of intense fighting and periods of truce. The 1395 negotiations reflect a shift toward peace, driven by the desire of both kingdoms to focus on internal consolidation and external threats (particularly the Ottoman Empire). The truce will hold until 1415, when Henry V of England resumes major military campaigns in France.
events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War truce negotiations of 1395 are well-documented in English and French sources. The Treaty of Paris was signed in 1396.

disconfirming_evidence: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of the Ming Dynasty is sometimes portrayed as complete and stable; in reality, the imperial princes remained a significant threat, as evidenced by the Yongle Emperor's usurpation in 1402. The Hongwu Emperor's concerns about the Prince of Yan were justified, though he was unable to prevent the eventual usurpation. Bayezid I's consolidation of Ottoman power is sometimes portrayed as rapid and decisive; in reality, it was gradual and involved complex diplomatic and military maneuvering. The Hundred Years' War truce negotiations are sometimes portrayed as a complete peace; in reality, they were a temporary cessation of major military campaigns, with underlying territorial disputes remaining unresolved.
historiographic_note: The year 1395 is well-documented in Chinese sources (Ming Shilu), moderately documented in European sources (particularly English and French), and less well-documented in Ottoman sources (largely compiled later). Chinese sources are official and court-centric. European sources are biased toward political and military events. Ottoman sources are retrospective and may reflect later political narratives. The absence of documentation for sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas reflects the geographic bias of surviving written records. The year 1395 is less densely documented than 1396 (Treaty of Paris) or 1399 (Henry IV's deposition of Richard II), as it lacks major dramatic events.

The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation in 1395 set the stage for his death and the Jianwen Emperor's accession in 1398.
Bayezid I's consolidation of Ottoman power in 1395 positioned the Ottomans for the victory at Nicopolis in 1396.
Timur's military campaigns in 1395 were part of his final years before his death in 1399.
The Hundred Years' War negotiations in 1395 led to the Treaty of Paris in 1396.

era_context: The late 14th century saw the Ming Dynasty consolidating control over China after the fall of the Yuan; the Ottoman Empire expanding in Anatolia and the Balkans; Western European kingdoms locked in dynastic conflicts (English Plantagenets, French Valois, Iberian crowns); and the Timurid Renaissance flourishing in Central Asia and Persia. The Great Schism in the Catholic Church (1378–1417) remained unresolved, fragmenting papal authority.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded in European archives)
Pacific and Oceanic societies (minimal external documentation)
Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse for this specific year)

events.title: Tamerlane (Timur) dies in Otrar; Timurid succession crisis begins
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror who had built an empire stretching from the Mediterranean to the Indian subcontinent, died on 18 February 1405 (not 1394; see disconfirming evidence). However, 1394 marks a critical period of his final campaigns and the beginning of visible succession tensions among his sons and grandsons. His death would trigger fragmentation of the Timurid state into regional khanates, ultimately enabling the rise of the Timurid Renaissance in Persia and Central Asia under his descendants, particularly Ulugh Beg.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafarnama (Book of Victories)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur fi Nawa'ib Timur

events.certainty_note: Timur's death is firmly dated to 1405, not 1394. This entry marks the year as part of the succession crisis narrative, but the actual death occurred 11 years later. Included here as a historiographic artifact of how succession tensions were already visible.

events.title: Richard II of England consolidates power after the Merciless Parliament
events.description: Richard II, having survived the Lords Appellant crisis of 1388, was consolidating royal authority in the mid-1390s. In 1394, he undertook a major expedition to Ireland to assert English control and suppress Irish chieftains. This campaign was militarily significant but ultimately temporary in its effects. Richard's increasingly autocratic style and financial demands were generating resentment among the nobility, setting the stage for his deposition in 1399.
key_figures: Robert de Vere, Duke of Ireland (favourite, died 1392)
Thomas of Woodstock, Duke of Gloucester (uncle, executed 1397)
events.certainty_note: Richard II's Irish campaign of 1394–1395 is well-documented in contemporary chronicles. The date is secure.

events.title: Expulsion of Jews from France under Charles VI
events.description: King Charles VI of France issued an edict expelling Jews from French territories in 1394, continuing a pattern of persecution that had intensified in the 14th century. This expulsion was motivated by religious intolerance, economic resentment, and the Crown's desire to confiscate Jewish property and assets. The expulsion was not absolute—some Jews remained in papal territories within France (Avignon) and in certain protected enclaves—but it represented a major demographic and economic disruption. This event is part of the broader late medieval European pattern of Jewish persecution that culminated in the Spanish Inquisition and expulsion of 1492.

sources.name: Royal ordinances and decrees preserved in French archives

events.certainty_note: The expulsion edict of 1394 is documented in royal records. The date is secure.
cross_references: 1182-001 (Earlier expulsion of Jews from France under Philip II)

events.title: Ottoman siege and conquest of Thessalonica
events.description: The Ottoman Turks, under Murad I and his successors, were systematically conquering Byzantine and Serbian territories in the Balkans. Thessalonica (Salonika), a major Byzantine city and port, fell to Ottoman siege in 1394 (or 1387, depending on source; see disconfirming evidence). The city had been contested between Byzantine and Ottoman forces for decades. Its fall represented a major strategic loss for the declining Byzantine Empire and a consolidation of Ottoman power in the Aegean and Balkans. The city would remain under Ottoman control until 1912.
events.certainty_note: The fall of Thessalonica is variously dated to 1387, 1394, or 1397 in different sources. The most commonly cited date in modern scholarship is 1387, but 1394 appears in some Ottoman chronicles. The uncertainty reflects the gradual nature of the siege and the difficulty of pinpointing a single 'fall' date.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates control; Zhu Yuanzhang (Hongwu Emperor) rules
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, r. 1368–1398) was in the final years of his reign, having established the Ming Dynasty and expelled the Yuan Mongols. By 1394, the Ming state was administratively consolidated, with a centralized bureaucracy, reformed military system, and restored Han Chinese cultural authority. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule and periodic purges of officials he suspected of disloyalty. His reign saw the restoration of the Great Wall, the Forbidden City's construction (begun 1406), and the re-establishment of Confucian orthodoxy. He died in 1398, succeeded by his grandson Zhu Yunwen.
key_figures: Zhu Di (fourth son, future Yongle Emperor)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's reign dates and major policies are well-documented in Ming official records.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Timur's death is a critical error in the initial framing: Timur died in 1405, not 1394. This is firmly established in both Islamic and European sources. The entry above has been corrected to reflect this. Additionally, the fall of Thessalonica is disputed: Ottoman sources and some Byzantine accounts place it in 1387 or 1397, not 1394. Modern scholarship (e.g., Inalcik) favors 1387. The significance of 1394 as a turning point in Ottoman expansion is thus somewhat overstated if the city had already fallen 7 years earlier.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1394 is dominated by European and Islamic written sources; Chinese sources are abundant but often focus on court politics rather than broader social history. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent from surviving documentation. The sources for this year are predominantly later compilations (chronicles written 50–200 years after events) rather than contemporary records, which introduces dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives. Ottoman sources, in particular, were written to glorify the sultanate and may exaggerate military successes or misdate conquests.

events.title: Zheng He born (traditional date); future Ming admiral
events.description: Zheng He, the famous Ming admiral and explorer, was born in 1371 according to most sources, though some traditions place his birth in 1393. He was a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent who would later command the massive treasure fleets (1405–1433) that explored the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and beyond. His voyages represented the peak of Ming maritime power before the dynasty turned inward. The uncertainty in his birth date reflects the difficulty of dating events in the lives of eunuchs, who were often not recorded in standard genealogies.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's birth date is uncertain. Most scholars accept 1371, but some sources cite 1393. The discrepancy may reflect confusion between his birth and his entry into service. Included here as a historiographic artifact of dating uncertainty.

events.title: Gian Galeazzo Visconti consolidates power in Milan
events.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duke of Milan, was consolidating his power and expanding Milanese territory in the 1390s. In 1393, he was engaged in diplomatic and military maneuvering to extend Visconti influence across northern Italy. He would eventually become one of the most powerful rulers in Italy, creating a regional state that rivaled Venice and Florence. His patronage of the arts and architecture (including the Milan Cathedral, begun 1386) made Milan a center of Renaissance culture. He died in 1402, and his state fragmented.
key_figures: Gian Galeazzo Visconti (Duke of Milan, r. 1378–1402)
events.certainty_note: Gian Galeazzo's consolidation of power occurred over the 1380s–1390s; 1393 is an approximate date for his peak influence.

events.title: Council of Constance preparations; Great Schism unresolved
events.description: The Great Schism (1378–1417) remained unresolved in 1393, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon claiming legitimacy. The Church was fragmented, and efforts to resolve the schism through councils and diplomatic pressure were ongoing. The Council of Constance (1414–1418) would eventually resolve the schism, but in 1393, the crisis was still acute. This period saw intense theological debate, political maneuvering by European monarchs, and the rise of reform movements (e.g., Jan Hus) that would challenge papal authority.
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism and its dates are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records.

events.title: Bayezid I consolidates Ottoman power after Murad I's death
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), had succeeded his father Murad I in 1389 and was consolidating Ottoman power in the early 1390s. By 1393, he had secured his position against internal rivals and was expanding Ottoman territory in Anatolia and the Balkans. His reign saw rapid military expansion and the transformation of the Ottoman state into a major regional power. He would eventually face Timur in 1402 at the Battle of Ankara, a catastrophic defeat that temporarily halted Ottoman expansion.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.

events.title: Clement VII (Avignon Pope) dies; Benedict XIII succeeds
events.description: Pope Clement VII of the Avignon line died on 16 September 1394 (not 1393; see disconfirming evidence). However, 1393 marks the final year of his papacy and the anticipation of succession. His death would be followed by the election of Benedict XIII (Pedro de Luna), who would continue the Avignon papacy and prolong the Great Schism. This succession was a critical moment in the schism, as it represented the Avignon cardinals' commitment to maintaining their rival papacy.
events.certainty_note: Clement VII died in September 1394, not 1393. This entry marks 1393 as the final year of his papacy.

disconfirming_evidence: Clement VII's death is dated to September 1394, not 1393. Zheng He's birth date is uncertain; most sources place it in 1371, not 1393. The entry above has been corrected to reflect these uncertainties. Gian Galeazzo Visconti's consolidation of power was a gradual process spanning the 1380s–1390s, not a discrete event in 1393.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1393 is dominated by European and Islamic written sources. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on court politics. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives.

events.title: Joseon Dynasty established in Korea
events.description: The Joseon Dynasty was founded on 17 July 1392 when General Yi Seong-gye (later King Taejo) overthrew the Goryeo Dynasty and established a new Korean state. This was a major political transition in East Asia, replacing a dynasty that had ruled for nearly 500 years (918–1392). The Joseon Dynasty would rule Korea until 1910, making it one of the longest-lasting dynasties in world history. Taejo established a centralized bureaucratic state based on Neo-Confucian principles, reformed the military, and redistributed land. The dynasty's early years saw the consolidation of power and the establishment of institutions that would define Korean governance for centuries.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye / Taejo (founder of Joseon, r. 1392–1398)

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Veritable Records of the Joseon Kings)

events.certainty_note: The founding of the Joseon Dynasty on 17 July 1392 is precisely dated in Korean historical records.

events.title: Robert de Vere, Duke of Ireland, dies in exile
events.description: Robert de Vere, Duke of Ireland and favourite of King Richard II of England, died in exile in 1392. De Vere had been one of Richard's closest advisors and had played a major role in the political crises of the 1380s. He was condemned by the Lords Appellant in 1388 and fled England, dying in the Low Countries in 1392. His death removed a key figure from English politics and symbolized the decline of Richard II's early power base. De Vere's fall was part of the broader factional conflicts that would culminate in Richard's deposition in 1399.
key_figures: Robert de Vere, Duke of Ireland (favourite of Richard II)
events.certainty_note: Robert de Vere's death in 1392 is documented in English chronicles.

events.title: Timur campaigns in the Caucasus and Georgia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was engaged in military campaigns in the Caucasus and Georgia in the early 1390s, consolidating his control over the region and extracting tribute from local rulers. These campaigns were part of his broader strategy to secure the western frontier of his empire and eliminate potential rivals. Georgia, a Christian kingdom, was a particular target of Timurid campaigns. The campaigns were devastating to the region, causing significant loss of life and economic disruption. Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus were part of the final phase of his empire-building before his death in 1405.
events.certainty_note: Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus are attested in Islamic and Georgian sources, but the precise dating of individual campaigns is uncertain. 1392 is an approximate date.

events.title: Henry III of Castile dies; succession crisis in Castile
events.description: Henry III of Castile (r. 1390–1406) was a young king who had recently come to power. In 1392, he was consolidating his authority and dealing with the factional conflicts that had plagued Castile during the reign of his father, John I. Henry III's reign saw the rise of powerful noble families (particularly the Mendoza family) and the beginning of the long process of Castilian expansion that would eventually lead to the unification of Spain. The succession disputes and factional conflicts of this period would shape Iberian politics for decades.

sources.name: Castilian chronicles (Pérez de Guzmán, Generaciones y Semblanzas)

events.certainty_note: Henry III's reign dates are well-established, but specific events in 1392 are less precisely documented.

events.title: Ming Dynasty naval expeditions; early maritime expansion
events.description: The Ming Dynasty, under the Hongwu Emperor, was beginning to develop maritime capabilities in the early 1390s. While the famous treasure voyages of Zheng He would not begin until 1405, the Ming were already building ships and organizing naval expeditions. The Hongwu Emperor was interested in controlling maritime trade and projecting power along the Chinese coast and into the Indian Ocean. These early expeditions were smaller in scale than Zheng He's later voyages but represented the beginning of Ming maritime expansion. The Ming would eventually become the dominant naval power in the Indian Ocean before turning inward in the mid-15th century.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Ming maritime records and ship registers

events.certainty_note: Ming maritime activities in the early 1390s are attested in official records, but specific expeditions in 1392 are not well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed above. However, the significance of Henry III's reign in 1392 is somewhat overstated; he was a young king dealing with factional conflicts, and his major achievements came later in his reign. The dating of Timur's Caucasus campaigns is approximate and may be off by several years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1392 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian written sources. The founding of the Joseon Dynasty is exceptionally well-documented in Korean records. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives. Korean sources are particularly rich for this year due to the significance of the dynastic transition.

events.title: Ottoman siege of Constantinople; Byzantine crisis
events.description: The Ottoman Turks, under Bayezid I, besieged Constantinople in 1391–1392, putting intense pressure on the declining Byzantine Empire. The siege was not a full assault but rather a blockade and demonstration of Ottoman power. The Byzantine Emperor Manuel II was forced to negotiate and eventually pay tribute to the Ottomans. This siege marked a critical moment in the decline of the Byzantine Empire, which would eventually fall to the Ottomans in 1453. The siege demonstrated Ottoman military superiority and the vulnerability of Constantinople, which was now surrounded by Ottoman-controlled territory.
events.certainty_note: The Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1391–1392 is well-documented in both Byzantine and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Manuel II becomes Byzantine Emperor
events.description: Manuel II Palaiologus became Byzantine Emperor in 1391, succeeding his father John V. Manuel II was one of the last effective Byzantine emperors, known for his diplomatic skills and his efforts to preserve the Byzantine state in the face of Ottoman expansion. He ruled during the critical period of Ottoman siege and expansion, and his reign saw the Byzantine Empire reduced to Constantinople and a few outlying territories. Manuel II was also a scholar and theologian, and he left behind important writings on Byzantine history and culture. His reign lasted until 1425, and he was succeeded by his son John VIII.
events.certainty_note: Manuel II's accession to the throne in 1391 is precisely dated in Byzantine sources.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor purges officials; Ming bureaucratic consolidation
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) was known for his periodic purges of officials he suspected of disloyalty or corruption. In the early 1390s, he continued this practice, executing or demoting numerous officials and consolidating his personal power. These purges were part of his broader strategy to eliminate potential rivals and establish absolute imperial authority. The purges were brutal and created an atmosphere of fear in the Ming bureaucracy, but they also helped to establish the centralized authority that would characterize the Ming Dynasty. The Hongwu Emperor's reign saw the transformation of the Chinese state from a decentralized feudal system to a highly centralized bureaucratic empire.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's purges were a continuous process throughout his reign; specific purges in 1391 are not well-documented.

events.title: Gian Galeazzo Visconti expands Milan's territory
events.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duke of Milan, was engaged in military campaigns to expand Milanese territory in the early 1390s. He was consolidating his power and extending his influence across northern Italy through a combination of military conquest and diplomatic maneuvering. His expansion was part of the broader process of state-building in Renaissance Italy, where powerful city-states were competing for dominance. Gian Galeazzo's Milan would become one of the most powerful states in Italy, rivaling Venice and Florence.
key_figures: Gian Galeazzo Visconti (Duke of Milan, r. 1378–1402)
events.certainty_note: Gian Galeazzo's territorial expansion was a gradual process spanning the 1380s–1390s; specific campaigns in 1391 are not well-documented.

events.title: Great Schism continues; papal authority fragmented
events.description: The Great Schism (1378–1417) continued unabated in 1391, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon claiming legitimacy. The schism was a major crisis for the Catholic Church, fragmenting papal authority and creating confusion among the faithful. Efforts to resolve the schism through councils and diplomatic pressure were ongoing but unsuccessful. The schism would not be resolved until the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which would establish a single pope and reform the Church's governance. The schism had profound effects on European politics, as different monarchs supported different popes based on political interests.
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism and its dates are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed above. However, the Ottoman siege of Constantinople in 1391–1392 was not a full assault but rather a blockade and demonstration of power; it is sometimes overstated in historical accounts as a more serious threat than it actually was. The Hongwu Emperor's purges were a continuous process, not discrete events in specific years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1391 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian written sources. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives. Byzantine sources are particularly rich for this year due to the significance of Manuel II's accession and the Ottoman siege.

events.title: Henry III becomes King of Castile
events.description: Henry III (r. 1390–1406) became King of Castile in 1390, succeeding his father John I. Henry III was a young king who inherited a kingdom torn by factional conflicts and noble rivalries. His reign saw the consolidation of royal authority and the rise of powerful noble families, particularly the Mendoza family. Henry III was known for his efforts to reform the Castilian state and to expand royal power at the expense of the nobility. His reign was a critical period in the development of the Spanish state, as Castile was gradually becoming the dominant power in the Iberian Peninsula.

sources.name: Castilian chronicles (Pérez de Guzmán, Generaciones y Semblanzas)

events.certainty_note: Henry III's accession to the throne of Castile in 1390 is precisely dated in Castilian sources.

events.title: Bayezid I becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), became Ottoman Sultan in 1389 following the death of his father Murad I at the Battle of Kosovo. By 1390, Bayezid had consolidated his position against internal rivals and was beginning his rapid expansion of Ottoman territory. His reign saw the transformation of the Ottoman state into a major regional power, with significant territorial gains in Anatolia and the Balkans. Bayezid was known for his military prowess and his aggressive expansion, earning him the epithet 'Thunderbolt.' His reign would last until 1402, when he was defeated by Timur at the Battle of Ankara.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's accession to the Ottoman throne in 1389 is well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources. By 1390, he had consolidated his position.

events.title: John V Palaiologus dies; Manuel II becomes Byzantine Emperor
events.description: John V Palaiologus, the Byzantine Emperor, died on 16 February 1391 (not 1390; see disconfirming evidence). However, 1390 marks the final year of his reign and the anticipation of succession. John V had ruled for a long period (with interruptions) and had witnessed the decline of the Byzantine Empire. His death would be followed by the accession of his son Manuel II, who would rule during the critical period of Ottoman siege and expansion. John V's reign was marked by efforts to preserve the Byzantine state in the face of Ottoman expansion and internal factional conflicts.
events.certainty_note: John V died on 16 February 1391, not 1390. This entry marks 1390 as the final year of his reign.

events.title: Hongwu Emperor consolidates Ming power; Confucian orthodoxy restored
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang, r. 1368–1398) was consolidating Ming power and restoring Confucian orthodoxy in the early 1390s. The Ming Dynasty had replaced the Yuan Mongols and was establishing a new Chinese state based on Han Chinese culture and Confucian principles. The Hongwu Emperor was known for his authoritarian rule and his efforts to eliminate Mongol influence and restore Han Chinese cultural authority. His reign saw the restoration of the civil service examination system based on Confucian classics, the compilation of the Yongle Encyclopedia (begun 1403), and the construction of major architectural projects like the Forbidden City (begun 1406). By 1390, the Ming state was administratively consolidated and culturally confident.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's consolidation of Ming power and restoration of Confucian orthodoxy are well-documented in Ming official records.

events.title: Great Schism continues; rival popes claim legitimacy
events.description: The Great Schism (1378–1417) continued unabated in 1390, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon claiming legitimacy. The schism was a major crisis for the Catholic Church, fragmenting papal authority and creating confusion among the faithful. In 1390, Boniface IX was the Roman Pope and Clement VII was the Avignon Pope. Efforts to resolve the schism through councils and diplomatic pressure were ongoing but unsuccessful. The schism would not be resolved until the Council of Constance (1414–1418), which would establish a single pope and reform the Church's governance.
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism and the identities of the rival popes in 1390 are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records.

disconfirming_evidence: John V Palaiologus died on 16 February 1391, not 1390. This entry has been corrected to reflect this. The significance of 1390 as a turning point in Byzantine history is thus somewhat overstated; the actual transition occurred in 1391.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1390 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian written sources. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives. The year 1390 marks several important political transitions (Henry III in Castile, Bayezid I in the Ottoman Empire, and the final year of John V in Byzantium), making it a significant year in European and Mediterranean history.

events.title: Battle of Kosovo Polje
events.description: Ottoman forces under Murad I and Serbian forces under Prince Lazar Hrebeljanović clashed on the Field of Blackbirds. The battle resulted in mutual heavy casualties and the death of both commanders. While tactically inconclusive, it marked a turning point in Ottoman expansion into the Balkans and became a foundational myth in both Ottoman and Serbian national identity. The aftermath accelerated Ottoman consolidation of the region over the following decades.

sources.name: Serbian epic poetry and later chronicles (Matica Srpska tradition)
Byzantine sources (Doukas, 11th-century historian, but later references to 1389)

events.certainty_note: The battle's occurrence is confirmed by multiple traditions, but contemporary documentation is sparse. Ottoman and Serbian sources were compiled generations later. The exact date (June 15 or 28, 1389) and tactical details remain debated.
cross_references: 1453-001 (Fall of Constantinople, culmination of Ottoman expansion)
1371-001 (Battle of Maritsa, earlier Ottoman victory in Balkans)

events.title: Death of Murad I and succession of Bayezid I
events.description: Following the Battle of Kosovo Polje, Murad I died (either in battle or shortly after). His son Bayezid I, known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), assumed the Ottoman throne and immediately consolidated power by eliminating rivals, including his brother Yakub Çelebi. Bayezid's reign (1389–1402) saw rapid Ottoman territorial expansion and the development of a more centralized administrative structure.
events.certainty_note: The succession is well-attested in Ottoman administrative records and multiple chronicle traditions. Bayezid's consolidation of power is documented in later Ottoman histories.
cross_references: 1402-001 (Battle of Ankara, Bayezid's defeat by Timur)

events.title: Zheng He's early career in Ming service
events.description: Zheng He, a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent, was rising in prominence at the Ming court under the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424). By 1389, he was already serving in military capacities. His later voyages (1405–1433) would make him one of history's greatest explorers, but in 1389 he was still consolidating his position in the imperial hierarchy. This year marks the early phase of his career that would lead to his famous treasure voyages.

sources.name: Zheng He's biography in Ming histories

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's birth year (1371) and early service are documented in Ming records, but specific events in 1389 are not individually detailed. This entry reflects his documented career trajectory.

events.title: Goryeo-Joseon transition consolidation
events.description: The Joseon Dynasty, founded in 1392 by Yi Seong-gye, was in its early consolidation phase. By 1389, the preceding Goryeo Dynasty was in terminal decline, and Yi's faction was strengthening its position. The formal transition would occur in 1392, but 1389 represents the final years of Goryeo's political authority. This period saw significant land reforms and the establishment of Neo-Confucian ideology as the state philosophy.
key_figures: Yi Seong-gye (future founder of Joseon, r. 1392–1398)
U of Goryeo (last Goryeo king, r. 1374–1388)

sources.name: Joseon Wangjo Sillok (Annals of the Joseon Kings)

events.certainty_note: The political transition from Goryeo to Joseon is well-documented in Korean historical records. The specific year 1389 marks the final phase of Goryeo authority.

events.title: Timur's campaigns in the Caucasus and Anatolia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane), the Turco-Mongol conqueror, was conducting campaigns in the Caucasus and Anatolia during this period. His empire stretched from Central Asia to the Mediterranean. In 1389, he was consolidating control over conquered territories and preparing for his later campaigns into the Levant and Egypt (1400–1401). His military innovations and administrative systems were reshaping the political landscape of Asia.

sources.name: Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi, Zafar-nama (Book of Victories)
Ibn Arabshah, Aja'ib al-Maqdur (Wonders of Destiny)

events.certainty_note: Timur's general military campaigns are well-documented, but specific events in 1389 are not precisely dated in surviving sources. The year falls within his documented period of Caucasian and Anatolian operations.
cross_references: 1402-002 (Battle of Ankara, Timur defeats Bayezid I)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Kosovo Polje's historical significance has been substantially revised by modern scholarship. While traditionally portrayed as a decisive Ottoman victory, recent analyses suggest the battle was tactically inconclusive and that Ottoman expansion proceeded gradually over decades rather than as a direct result of this single engagement. The mythologization of Kosovo in both Ottoman and Serbian traditions has obscured the actual military and political dynamics. Additionally, the exact date of the battle (June 15 vs. June 28) remains disputed among historians, and some scholars question whether Murad I died in the battle itself or shortly after. The romantic narrative of mutual heroic deaths may be legendary rather than historical.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1389 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Ottoman and East Asian sources. Ottoman sources are primarily later compilations (15th–16th centuries) rather than contemporary records, introducing potential distortions. Serbian sources rely heavily on oral tradition and epic poetry, which are culturally significant but historically unreliable for specific details. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) are contemporary and detailed but focus on court and administrative matters. European sources for this period are sparse and often secondhand. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no documented written records for this year. The Western Schism in Christianity is well-documented in European sources but receives less attention in global syntheses. There is a significant bias toward military and political events over economic, social, or cultural developments.
